title: Orbital Cellulitis
oneLiner: A sight- and life-threatening infection of the post-septal orbital tissues — suspect it in any child with unilateral periorbital swelling, fever, and restricted eye movement.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**
A 6-year-old boy presents with 2 days of worsening right eyelid swelling, redness, and fever. Mum says he had a runny nose and sinus congestion last week. On examination, his right eye is nearly swollen shut, conjunctiva is chemotic, and he complains of pain when moving the eye. The pupil is reactive, but you notice mild proptosis. He is febrile (39.0°C) and tachycardic. He refuses to open the eye spontaneously.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Unilateral eyelid swelling & erythema** → inflammation/infection in periorbital region; consider preseptal vs. orbital cellulitis.
- **Fever** → systemic infection; not always present in preseptal.
- **Recent sinusitis/respiratory infection** → common antecedent: ethmoid sinusitis spreads through lamina papyracea.
- **Painful, restricted eye movement** → key differentiating sign: infection has entered the orbit (postseptal), causing myositis or mass effect.
- **Chemosis** → conjunctival edema from venous congestion and inflammation.
- **Proptosis** → increased orbital volume from pus or oedema; confirms orbital involvement.
- **Refusal to open eye** → photophobia or pain; also seen in severe inflammation.
- **Age 6** → peak age for orbital cellulitis (5–12 years); also in adults with sinusitis or trauma.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Neonate (<1 year old)** — may present only with fever, poor feeding, and subtle lid swelling; no clear sinusitis history. Often from dacryocystitis (nasolacrimal duct obstruction). Requires high index of suspicion.
- **Adult with penetrating trauma** — no sinus symptoms; orbital wall fracture or foreign body. Signs may be masked by concomitant eyelid trauma.
- **Immunocompromised patient** — may present without fever, less swelling, but rapid progression to cavernous sinus thrombosis. Aspergillus (fungal) orbital cellulitis is a consideration in diabetics/neutropenics: pain out of proportion, black necrotic eschar on palate or nose.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Definition:** Orbital cellulitis is an infection of the soft tissues *behind* the orbital septum (i.e. within the bony orbit) — involving fat, extraocular muscles, and sometimes the optic nerve. It is distinct from preseptal cellulitis (infection anterior to the septum), which is far more common and benign.

**Why it matters:**
- **Stakes are high** — blindness from optic nerve compression or ischaemia, cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, cerebral abscess. Mortality from cavernous sinus thrombosis can reach 30%.
- **Common pitfall** — misdiagnosing as preseptal cellulitis because postseptal signs are subtle early on. A missed orbital cellulitis that progresses to vision loss is a medico-legal catastrophe.
- **Frequency** — ~2-3% of paediatric periorbital infections are orbital; in adults, less common but more aggressive.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
The story begins in the **paranasal sinuses**, most often the ethmoid sinuses in children (they are the smallest and have the thinnest bony wall — the lamina papyracea). In adults, frontal and maxillary sinuses are more commonly implicated.

- **Step 1: Sinusitis** → mucosal swelling, ostial obstruction, bacterial overgrowth (Streptococcus pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Staph. aureus, anaerobes).
- **Step 2: Spread into orbit** — direct extension through congenital dehiscences in lamina papyracea, or via thrombophlebitis of valveless veins (the ophthalmic veins communicate freely with sinus veins).
- **Step 3: Inflammatory response** — oedema, pus, and congestion within the confined bony orbit (volume ~30 mL). This causes:
  - **Proptosis** → forward displacement of globe due to mass.
  - **Painful eye movement** → infection/inflammation of extraocular muscles (myositis) and their sheaths.
  - **Chemosis** → conjunctival oedema from venous outflow obstruction.
  - **Eyelid swelling** → secondary to anterior spread of inflammation across the septum (but note: postseptal process can present with lid swelling that mimics preseptal).
- **Step 4: Compartment syndrome** → orbital pressure rises → compromises blood flow to optic nerve (ischaemic optic neuropathy) and retina (central retinal artery occlusion). **This is the emergency.**
- **Step 5: Retrograde extension** → infection can travel via superior and inferior ophthalmic veins to the cavernous sinus → septic thrombophlebitis → cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), contralateral eye involvement, meningitis, and abscess.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
- *Restricted eye movement* = extraocular muscle inflammation and/or mass effect.
- *Proptosis* = increased orbital volume; see it early in paediatric orbital cellulitis, but can be subtle (measure with Hertel exophthalmometer).
- *Fever* = systemic bacterial infection; higher grade of fever more likely with postseptal disease.
- *Refusal to open eye* = photophobia due to meningeal irritation (if meningitis) or simply pain from eye movement.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Most common cause | Stem clue |
|----------|------------------|-----------|
| **Infectious – Sinogenic** (60-80%) | Ethmoid sinusitis | "Recent upper respiratory infection / sinus congestion" |
| **Infectious – Trauma** (10-20%) | Penetrating orbital injury (e.g., wooden foreign body) | "Fell onto a stick" or "eye injury with gardening / construction" |
| **Infectious – Haematogenous** (rare) | Bacteraemia (Staph. aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes) | "Child with recent varicella / scarlet fever + periorbital swelling" |
| **Infectious – Dacryocystitis** (neonates) | Nasolacrimal duct obstruction (Staph. aureus, Streptococcus) | "Neonate with epiphora, swelling at medial canthus" |
| **Infectious – Fungal** (adults, immunocompromised) | Mucorales (mucormycosis), Aspergillus | "Diabetic in ketoacidosis with black eschar on palate / nose" |
| **Inflammatory (non-infectious)** | Thyroid eye disease, Idiopathic orbital inflammation | "Painless proptosis, no fever, labs show thyroid autoantibodies" |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **contiguous spread from acute sinusitis** (especially ethmoid). In children up to 90% have concurrent sinusitis.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** None formally required, but **Chandler classification** stages orbital cellulitis (I–V) used to guide management. However, for clinical practice, the key is **CT imaging** to confirm postseptal involvement.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Full blood count** | White cell count, neutrophils | Inflammation marker | High WCC >15 ± left shift supports bacterial infection; normal does not rule out (especially immunocompromised) |
| **C-reactive protein (CRP)** | Acute phase reactant | Monitor severity and response to treatment | Usually >50–100 mg/L; falling CRP after 48 hours indicates treatment working |
| **Blood cultures** | Pathogen in blood | Rule out bacteraemia; guide antibiotics | Positive in ~30-50% of orbital cellulitis; often S. pneumoniae, S. aureus |
| **CT orbit with contrast** | Soft tissue window: shows postseptal fat stranding, proptosis, subperiosteal abscess, sinus opacification | **Gold standard** to confirm orbital involvement and differentiate preseptal vs. orbital | Any finding of soft tissue swelling *posterior to septum* = orbital cellulitis. Subperiosteal abscess = stage III. |
| **MRI orbit (if suspected intracranial extension)** | Better for cavernous sinus thrombosis, dural sinus involvement, brain abscess | If signs of further spread (contralateral deficits, meningism) | High sensitivity for thrombus; T1 post-contrast fat-sat |
| **Lumbar puncture** (only if meningeal signs) | CSF analysis | Suspect meningitis | WBC >5, protein elevated, glucose low; culture may yield same organism |
| **Nasal/sinus swab** (guided) | Microbiology of sinus | If prior antibiotics failed, or atypical presentation | May identify MRSA or fungi |

**Pattern recognition:**
- **Confirm / rule out:** CT orbit showing **postseptal inflammation** (fat stranding, abscess) confirms orbital cellulitis. If no postseptal changes → preseptal cellulitis (managed with oral antibiotics, not urgent IV).
- **Single finding that should raise alarm:** Proptosis >2mm from contralateral eye OR restriction of EOM in any direction.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Assess for red flags:** vision loss, relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD), loss of colour vision, severe proptosis → call senior immediately for orbital decompression (canthotomy/cantholysis) if tension orbit.
- **Start IV antibiotics** — cover sinus flora (Strep spp., Staph. aureus, anaerobes, H. influenzae). Typical regimen:
  - **Co-amoxiclav:** 1.2 g IV QDS (child: 30 mg/kg/dose TDS) — first line in many UK centres.
  - **Alternative:** Cefuroxime 1.5 g IV TDS (child: 50 mg/kg/dose TDS) PLUS Metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS (child: 7.5 mg/kg/dose TDS) — if penicillin allergy or local resistance.
  - **If MRSA risk** (IVDU, known carriage): add Vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV BD (use trough levels).
- **Urgent CT orbit** — must not delay antibiotics; do simultaneously.
- **Elevate head of bed** — reduce orbital venous pressure.
- **Analgesia** — paracetamol 1 g IV QDS (child: 15 mg/kg) ± NSAIDs if no contraindication.

**Ward (24-72 hours):**
- **Monitor vision** — every 4 hours: visual acuity, colour vision (red desaturation), RAPD, pupils, eye movements. Document.
- **Repeat CRP daily** — should fall; if rising or not improving by 48h, consider imaging (repeat CT) for abscess formation.
- **Surgical drainage** — indicated for:
  - Subperiosteal or orbital abscess (Chandler III–V)
  - No improvement after 48h of IV antibiotics
  - Vision loss or RAPD
  - Clinical deterioration (worsening proptosis, fever)
- **ENT review** — for concurrent sinus drainage (endoscopic sinus surgery) – often done together with ophthalmology.
- **Switch to oral antibiotics** — once afebrile for 24h, CRP improving, and able to tolerate oral. Usually 7–14 days total course. Example: co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS (child: 30 mg/kg/dose TDS).

**Long-term:**
- **Outpatient follow-up** — ophthalmology review in 2 weeks to check for residual strabismus, amblyopia (children), or optic atrophy.
- **Recurrence prevention** — sinus disease management: nasal saline rinses, intranasal corticosteroids, allergy control; consider ENT referral for chronic sinusitis.

**What NOT to do:**
- ❌ **Do not give oral antibiotics as first line** — orbital cellulitis needs high-dose IV to penetrate the orbit.
- ❌ **Do not use topical antibiotics alone** — they don’t reach postseptal space.
- ❌ **Do not delay CT for fear of radiation** — the risk of blindness is greater.
- ❌ **Do not use corticosteroids** unless after definitive surgical drainage and ongoing inflammation — they can mask deterioration.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Orbital Cellulitis | Preseptal Cellulitis | Idiopathic Orbital Inflammation (Orbital Pseudotumor) | Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Pain** | ++, especially with eye movement | + lid tenderness, no eye pain | + dull ache, worse with eye movement | ++ severe headache, periorbital pain |
| **Fever** | +++ (high) | ± low-grade | Absent | ++ high fever, chills |
| **Proptosis** | Yes (often mild-moderate) | No | Yes, often moderate-severe | May be present (bilateral late) |
| **EOM restriction** | Yes, painful | No | Yes, may be painless or painful | Yes (CN III/IV/VI palsy) |
| **Pupil / vision** | RAPD, vision loss in stages IV-V | Normal | Occasionally diplopia, rarely vision loss | Cranial nerve deficits (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), pupillary involvement |
| **CN involvement** | Uncommon early | None | None | Yes – multiple cranial nerves |
| **Bilateral** | Rare, suggests cavernous sinus thrombosis | No | Yes, can be bilateral | Yes (contralateral signs) |
| **Laboratory** | WCC ↑, CRP ↑↑ | Moderate WCC ↑ | CRP normal or mildly elevated (ANA, RF may be +) | WCC ↑, CRP ↑↑, D-dimer ↑ (thrombus) |
| **Imaging key** | Postseptal fat stranding, sinusitis, abscess | Lid swelling anterior to septum | Diffuse enhancement of orbital tissues, no abscess | Filling defect in cavernous sinus (CT venogram) |
| **Key differentiator** | CT shows infection posterior to septum | No postseptal involvement on CT | No fever, no sinusitis, no abscess, responds to steroids | Contralateral CN signs, proptosis, rapid deterioration |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Optic neuropathy / blindness** | Painless vision loss (or rapid decline), relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD), loss of red colour perception | *RAPD* is the single most important sign — test with swinging flashlight. Severe proptosis >5mm | **Emergency orbital decompression** (canthotomy/cantholysis) + IV methylprednisolone if no contraindication. Urgent ophthalmology + neurosurgery. |
| **Subperiosteal abscess** | Worsening proptosis, EOM restriction, failure to improve after 48h IV antibiotics | CT shows crescent-shaped collection along medial orbital wall | Drainage: endoscopic or transcutaneous. Usually see in children >9 years; smaller abscesses (<1 cm) may be trialled on IV antibiotics alone if vision normal. |
| **Cavernous sinus thrombosis** | High fever, headache, bilateral periorbital oedema, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), seizures, altered consciousness | Contralateral eye involvement is pathognomonic. Meningism may develop. | IV heparin + IV antibiotics (broad spectrum, consider MRSA/anaerobes). ICU for monitoring. MRI venogram. |
| **Meningitis** | Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, vomiting, Kernig's sign positive | Lumbar puncture: elevated WCC, low glucose | IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin; imaging for abscess. |
| **Intracranial abscess** | Focal neurological deficits, raised ICP (headache, papillodema), seizures | CT/MRI shows ring-enhancing lesion | Neurosurgical drainage + long-term antibiotics (4–6 weeks). |

**Late-presentation pattern:**
A child presents with a history of “pink eye” treated with topical antibiotics for 3 days, now with severe headache, vomiting, and left-sided weakness. The stem wants you to think **cavernous sinus thrombosis** or **cerebral abscess** from missed orbital cellulitis.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: “CELLULITIS” for orbital features**
- **C** – Chemosis
- **E** – EOM restriction (painful)
- **L** – Lid swelling (but look beyond)
- **L** – Loss of vision (late)
- **U** – Unilateral (usually; bilateral suggests cavernous sinus involvement)
- **L** – Leukocytosis / fever
- **I** – Increased proptosis
- **T** – Tenderness on eye movement
- **I** – Imaging (CT orbit) confirms
- **S** – Sinusitis (common trigger)

**Pearls:**
1. **Preseptal vs Orbital: the septum is the line.** If you can see the globe still (some movement), it may still be orbital. The only definitive way is CT, but clinically: **no proptosis + full EOM = preseptal** until proven otherwise.
2. **Red desaturation is the earliest vision-threatening sign.** Test with a red bottle top: patient compares same eye each time. As soon as desaturation is reported, consider urgent imaging/decompression.
3. **In children, orbital cellulitis is almost always secondary to sinusitis.** If no sinusitis, suspect trauma.
4. **Do not be reassured by “mild” proptosis.** The orbit is small; a few mm of proptosis indicates significant mass. Document measurement.
5. **Failure to improve after 48h of IV antibiotics = surgical indication.** Do not wait longer.
6. **In adults, especially diabetics, consider fungal rhinosinusitis (mucormycosis).** Pain out of proportion, nasal necrosis, black eschar. Call ENT and ID immediately.
7. **Cavernous sinus thrombosis: look for signs of contralateral eye involvement.** That’s the hallmark.
8. **Always document visual acuity and pupils** on admission and every review — medicolegally crucial.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking chemosis for allergic conjunctivitis — check for proptosis and EOM.
- Prescribing oral antibiotics for a child with periorbital swelling and fever without performing EOM assessment — children may not cooperate; use gentle distraction with a toy to elicit movement.
- Not calling ophthalmology immediately if RAPD detected.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ophthalmology / ICU:**

| Condition | Trigger | Action |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| **RAPD or vision loss** | Relative afferent pupillary defect or patient reports vision loss (<6/60 or 20/200) | **Immediate: call ophthalmology (senior reg or consultant) for possible decompression. Also call ENT. Do not wait for CT.** |
| **Severe proptosis** | Proptosis >5mm (using Hertel) or inability to close eyelids | Urgent ophthalmology review – may need canthotomy. |
| **No improvement after 48h IV antibiotics** | Persistent fever, no decrease in CRP, worsening proptosis | Repeat CT; consider surgical drainage (ophthalmology + ENT). |
| **Suspected cavernous sinus thrombosis** | New cranial nerve palsy (any of III, IV, VI), bilateral signs, seizures, GCS <15 | **Call ICU.** Start IV heparin (if no contraindication), broad-spectrum antibiotics, neuroimaging (MRI/MRV). |
| **Meningism / altered consciousness** | Neck stiffness, photophobia, GCS drop | **Call ICU and neurology.** Lumbar puncture after CT. Consider starting ceftriaxone + vancomycin. |
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥5 (e.g., RR>24, SpO2<92%, HR>110, SBP<90) | Escalate to medical/surgical registrar. |
| **Lactate** | >2.0 mmol/L with signs of sepsis | Sepsis 6 bundle; inform ICU if >4. |
| **GCS** | ≤13 or drop by 2 points | **Crash call** – possible raised ICP or intracranial complication. |

**Bottom line for the FY1:** If you see a child with periorbital swelling and any hint of restricted eye movement or proptosis, you do not send them home with oral antibiotics. You start IV antibiotics, get a CT, and speak to ophthalmology. That single decision changes outcome.

rawMarkdown:
## Orbital Cellulitis
**One-liner:** A sight- and life-threatening infection of the post-septal orbital tissues — suspect it in any child with unilateral periorbital swelling, fever, and restricted eye movement.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**
A 6-year-old boy presents with 2 days of worsening right eyelid swelling, redness, and fever. Mum says he had a runny nose and sinus congestion last week. On examination, his right eye is nearly swollen shut, conjunctiva is chemotic, and he complains of pain when moving the eye. The pupil is reactive, but you notice mild proptosis. He is febrile (39.0°C) and tachycardic. He refuses to open the eye spontaneously.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Unilateral eyelid swelling & erythema** → inflammation/infection in periorbital region; consider preseptal vs. orbital cellulitis.
- **Fever** → systemic infection; not always present in preseptal.
- **Recent sinusitis/respiratory infection** → common antecedent: ethmoid sinusitis spreads through lamina papyracea.
- **Painful, restricted eye movement** → key differentiating sign: infection has entered the orbit (postseptal), causing myositis or mass effect.
- **Chemosis** → conjunctival edema from venous congestion and inflammation.
- **Proptosis** → increased orbital volume from pus or oedema; confirms orbital involvement.
- **Refusal to open eye** → photophobia or pain; also seen in severe inflammation.
- **Age 6** → peak age for orbital cellulitis (5–12 years); also in adults with sinusitis or trauma.

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Neonate (<1 year old)** — may present only with fever, poor feeding, and subtle lid swelling; no clear sinusitis history. Often from dacryocystitis (nasolacrimal duct obstruction). Requires high index of suspicion.
- **Adult with penetrating trauma** — no sinus symptoms; orbital wall fracture or foreign body. Signs may be masked by concomitant eyelid trauma.
- **Immunocompromised patient** — may present without fever, less swelling, but rapid progression to cavernous sinus thrombosis. Aspergillus (fungal) orbital cellulitis is a consideration in diabetics/neutropenics: pain out of proportion, black necrotic eschar on palate or nose.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Definition:** Orbital cellulitis is an infection of the soft tissues *behind* the orbital septum (i.e. within the bony orbit) — involving fat, extraocular muscles, and sometimes the optic nerve. It is distinct from preseptal cellulitis (infection anterior to the septum), which is far more common and benign.

**Why it matters:**
- **Stakes are high** — blindness from optic nerve compression or ischaemia, cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, cerebral abscess. Mortality from cavernous sinus thrombosis can reach 30%.
- **Common pitfall** — misdiagnosing as preseptal cellulitis because postseptal signs are subtle early on. A missed orbital cellulitis that progresses to vision loss is a medico-legal catastrophe.
- **Frequency** — ~2-3% of paediatric periorbital infections are orbital; in adults, less common but more aggressive.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

The story begins in the **paranasal sinuses**, most often the ethmoid sinuses in children (they are the smallest and have the thinnest bony wall — the lamina papyracea). In adults, frontal and maxillary sinuses are more commonly implicated.

- **Step 1: Sinusitis** → mucosal swelling, ostial obstruction, bacterial overgrowth (Streptococcus pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Staph. aureus, anaerobes).
- **Step 2: Spread into orbit** — direct extension through congenital dehiscences in lamina papyracea, or via thrombophlebitis of valveless veins (the ophthalmic veins communicate freely with sinus veins).
- **Step 3: Inflammatory response** — oedema, pus, and congestion within the confined bony orbit (volume ~30 mL). This causes:
  - **Proptosis** → forward displacement of globe due to mass.
  - **Painful eye movement** → infection/inflammation of extraocular muscles (myositis) and their sheaths.
  - **Chemosis** → conjunctival oedema from venous outflow obstruction.
  - **Eyelid swelling** → secondary to anterior spread of inflammation across the septum (but note: postseptal process can present with lid swelling that mimics preseptal).
- **Step 4: Compartment syndrome** → orbital pressure rises → compromises blood flow to optic nerve (ischaemic optic neuropathy) and retina (central retinal artery occlusion). **This is the emergency.**
- **Step 5: Retrograde extension** → infection can travel via superior and inferior ophthalmic veins to the cavernous sinus → septic thrombophlebitis → cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), contralateral eye involvement, meningitis, and abscess.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:**
- *Restricted eye movement* = extraocular muscle inflammation and/or mass effect.
- *Proptosis* = increased orbital volume; see it early in paediatric orbital cellulitis, but can be subtle (measure with Hertel exophthalmometer).
- *Fever* = systemic bacterial infection; higher grade of fever more likely with postseptal disease.
- *Refusal to open eye* = photophobia due to meningeal irritation (if meningitis) or simply pain from eye movement.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Most common cause | Stem clue |
|----------|------------------|-----------|
| **Infectious – Sinogenic** (60-80%) | Ethmoid sinusitis | "Recent upper respiratory infection / sinus congestion" |
| **Infectious – Trauma** (10-20%) | Penetrating orbital injury (e.g., wooden foreign body) | "Fell onto a stick" or "eye injury with gardening / construction" |
| **Infectious – Haematogenous** (rare) | Bacteraemia (Staph. aureus, Streptococcus pyogenes) | "Child with recent varicella / scarlet fever + periorbital swelling" |
| **Infectious – Dacryocystitis** (neonates) | Nasolacrimal duct obstruction (Staph. aureus, Streptococcus) | "Neonate with epiphora, swelling at medial canthus" |
| **Infectious – Fungal** (adults, immunocompromised) | Mucorales (mucormycosis), Aspergillus | "Diabetic in ketoacidosis with black eschar on palate / nose" |
| **Inflammatory (non-infectious)** | Thyroid eye disease, Idiopathic orbital inflammation | "Painless proptosis, no fever, labs show thyroid autoantibodies" |

**Highlight:** The most common cause is **contiguous spread from acute sinusitis** (especially ethmoid). In children up to 90% have concurrent sinusitis.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** None formally required, but **Chandler classification** stages orbital cellulitis (I–V) used to guide management. However, for clinical practice, the key is **CT imaging** to confirm postseptal involvement.

| Investigation | What it shows | Why order | Interpretation |
|---------------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Full blood count** | White cell count, neutrophils | Inflammation marker | High WCC >15 ± left shift supports bacterial infection; normal does not rule out (especially immunocompromised) |
| **C-reactive protein (CRP)** | Acute phase reactant | Monitor severity and response to treatment | Usually >50–100 mg/L; falling CRP after 48 hours indicates treatment working |
| **Blood cultures** | Pathogen in blood | Rule out bacteraemia; guide antibiotics | Positive in ~30-50% of orbital cellulitis; often S. pneumoniae, S. aureus |
| **CT orbit with contrast** | Soft tissue window: shows postseptal fat stranding, proptosis, subperiosteal abscess, sinus opacification | **Gold standard** to confirm orbital involvement and differentiate preseptal vs. orbital | Any finding of soft tissue swelling *posterior to septum* = orbital cellulitis. Subperiosteal abscess = stage III. |
| **MRI orbit (if suspected intracranial extension)** | Better for cavernous sinus thrombosis, dural sinus involvement, brain abscess | If signs of further spread (contralateral deficits, meningism) | High sensitivity for thrombus; T1 post-contrast fat-sat |
| **Lumbar puncture** (only if meningeal signs) | CSF analysis | Suspect meningitis | WBC >5, protein elevated, glucose low; culture may yield same organism |
| **Nasal/sinus swab** (guided) | Microbiology of sinus | If prior antibiotics failed, or atypical presentation | May identify MRSA or fungi |

**Pattern recognition:**
- **Confirm / rule out:** CT orbit showing **postseptal inflammation** (fat stranding, abscess) confirms orbital cellulitis. If no postseptal changes → preseptal cellulitis (managed with oral antibiotics, not urgent IV).
- **Single finding that should raise alarm:** Proptosis >2mm from contralateral eye OR restriction of EOM in any direction.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Assess for red flags:** vision loss, relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD), loss of colour vision, severe proptosis → call senior immediately for orbital decompression (canthotomy/cantholysis) if tension orbit.
- **Start IV antibiotics** — cover sinus flora (Strep spp., Staph. aureus, anaerobes, H. influenzae). Typical regimen:
  - **Co-amoxiclav:** 1.2 g IV QDS (child: 30 mg/kg/dose TDS) — first line in many UK centres.
  - **Alternative:** Cefuroxime 1.5 g IV TDS (child: 50 mg/kg/dose TDS) PLUS Metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS (child: 7.5 mg/kg/dose TDS) — if penicillin allergy or local resistance.
  - **If MRSA risk** (IVDU, known carriage): add Vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV BD (use trough levels).
- **Urgent CT orbit** — must not delay antibiotics; do simultaneously.
- **Elevate head of bed** — reduce orbital venous pressure.
- **Analgesia** — paracetamol 1 g IV QDS (child: 15 mg/kg) ± NSAIDs if no contraindication.

**Ward (24-72 hours):**
- **Monitor vision** — every 4 hours: visual acuity, colour vision (red desaturation), RAPD, pupils, eye movements. Document.
- **Repeat CRP daily** — should fall; if rising or not improving by 48h, consider imaging (repeat CT) for abscess formation.
- **Surgical drainage** — indicated for:
  - Subperiosteal or orbital abscess (Chandler III–V)
  - No improvement after 48h of IV antibiotics
  - Vision loss or RAPD
  - Clinical deterioration (worsening proptosis, fever)
- **ENT review** — for concurrent sinus drainage (endoscopic sinus surgery) – often done together with ophthalmology.
- **Switch to oral antibiotics** — once afebrile for 24h, CRP improving, and able to tolerate oral. Usually 7–14 days total course. Example: co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS (child: 30 mg/kg/dose TDS).

**Long-term:**
- **Outpatient follow-up** — ophthalmology review in 2 weeks to check for residual strabismus, amblyopia (children), or optic atrophy.
- **Recurrence prevention** — sinus disease management: nasal saline rinses, intranasal corticosteroids, allergy control; consider ENT referral for chronic sinusitis.

**What NOT to do:**
- ❌ **Do not give oral antibiotics as first line** — orbital cellulitis needs high-dose IV to penetrate the orbit.
- ❌ **Do not use topical antibiotics alone** — they don’t reach postseptal space.
- ❌ **Do not delay CT for fear of radiation** — the risk of blindness is greater.
- ❌ **Do not use corticosteroids** unless after definitive surgical drainage and ongoing inflammation — they can mask deterioration.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Orbital Cellulitis | Preseptal Cellulitis | Idiopathic Orbital Inflammation (Orbital Pseudotumor) | Cavernous Sinus Thrombosis |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Pain** | ++, especially with eye movement | + lid tenderness, no eye pain | + dull ache, worse with eye movement | ++ severe headache, periorbital pain |
| **Fever** | +++ (high) | ± low-grade | Absent | ++ high fever, chills |
| **Proptosis** | Yes (often mild-moderate) | No | Yes, often moderate-severe | May be present (bilateral late) |
| **EOM restriction** | Yes, painful | No | Yes, may be painless or painful | Yes (CN III/IV/VI palsy) |
| **Pupil / vision** | RAPD, vision loss in stages IV-V | Normal | Occasionally diplopia, rarely vision loss | Cranial nerve deficits (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), pupillary involvement |
| **CN involvement** | Uncommon early | None | None | Yes – multiple cranial nerves |
| **Bilateral** | Rare, suggests cavernous sinus thrombosis | No | Yes, can be bilateral | Yes (contralateral signs) |
| **Laboratory** | WCC ↑, CRP ↑↑ | Moderate WCC ↑ | CRP normal or mildly elevated (ANA, RF may be +) | WCC ↑, CRP ↑↑, D-dimer ↑ (thrombus) |
| **Imaging key** | Postseptal fat stranding, sinusitis, abscess | Lid swelling anterior to septum | Diffuse enhancement of orbital tissues, no abscess | Filling defect in cavernous sinus (CT venogram) |
| **Key differentiator** | CT shows infection posterior to septum | No postseptal involvement on CT | No fever, no sinusitis, no abscess, responds to steroids | Contralateral CN signs, proptosis, rapid deterioration |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | How it presents | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|----------------|-----------|------------|
| **Optic neuropathy / blindness** | Painless vision loss (or rapid decline), relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD), loss of red colour perception | *RAPD* is the single most important sign — test with swinging flashlight. Severe proptosis >5mm | **Emergency orbital decompression** (canthotomy/cantholysis) + IV methylprednisolone if no contraindication. Urgent ophthalmology + neurosurgery. |
| **Subperiosteal abscess** | Worsening proptosis, EOM restriction, failure to improve after 48h IV antibiotics | CT shows crescent-shaped collection along medial orbital wall | Drainage: endoscopic or transcutaneous. Usually see in children >9 years; smaller abscesses (<1 cm) may be trialled on IV antibiotics alone if vision normal. |
| **Cavernous sinus thrombosis** | High fever, headache, bilateral periorbital oedema, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), seizures, altered consciousness | Contralateral eye involvement is pathognomonic. Meningism may develop. | IV heparin + IV antibiotics (broad spectrum, consider MRSA/anaerobes). ICU for monitoring. MRI venogram. |
| **Meningitis** | Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, vomiting, Kernig's sign positive | Lumbar puncture: elevated WCC, low glucose | IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin; imaging for abscess. |
| **Intracranial abscess** | Focal neurological deficits, raised ICP (headache, papillodema), seizures | CT/MRI shows ring-enhancing lesion | Neurosurgical drainage + long-term antibiotics (4–6 weeks). |

**Late-presentation pattern:**
A child presents with a history of “pink eye” treated with topical antibiotics for 3 days, now with severe headache, vomiting, and left-sided weakness. The stem wants you to think **cavernous sinus thrombosis** or **cerebral abscess** from missed orbital cellulitis.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: “CELLULITIS” for orbital features**
- **C** – Chemosis
- **E** – EOM restriction (painful)
- **L** – Lid swelling (but look beyond)
- **L** – Loss of vision (late)
- **U** – Unilateral (usually; bilateral suggests cavernous sinus involvement)
- **L** – Leukocytosis / fever
- **I** – Increased proptosis
- **T** – Tenderness on eye movement
- **I** – Imaging (CT orbit) confirms
- **S** – Sinusitis (common trigger)

**Pearls:**
1. **Preseptal vs Orbital: the septum is the line.** If you can see the globe still (some movement), it may still be orbital. The only definitive way is CT, but clinically: **no proptosis + full EOM = preseptal** until proven otherwise.
2. **Red desaturation is the earliest vision-threatening sign.** Test with a red bottle top: patient compares same eye each time. As soon as desaturation is reported, consider urgent imaging/decompression.
3. **In children, orbital cellulitis is almost always secondary to sinusitis.** If no sinusitis, suspect trauma.
4. **Do not be reassured by “mild” proptosis.** The orbit is small; a few mm of proptosis indicates significant mass. Document measurement.
5. **Failure to improve after 48h of IV antibiotics = surgical indication.** Do not wait longer.
6. **In adults, especially diabetics, consider fungal rhinosinusitis (mucormycosis).** Pain out of proportion, nasal necrosis, black eschar. Call ENT and ID immediately.
7. **Cavernous sinus thrombosis: look for signs of contralateral eye involvement.** That’s the hallmark.
8. **Always document visual acuity and pupils** on admission and every review — medicolegally crucial.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking chemosis for allergic conjunctivitis — check for proptosis and EOM.
- Prescribing oral antibiotics for a child with periorbital swelling and fever without performing EOM assessment — children may not cooperate; use gentle distraction with a toy to elicit movement.
- Not calling ophthalmology immediately if RAPD detected.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ophthalmology / ICU:**

| Condition | Trigger | Action |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| **RAPD or vision loss** | Relative afferent pupillary defect or patient reports vision loss (<6/60 or 20/200) | **Immediate: call ophthalmology (senior reg or consultant) for possible decompression. Also call ENT. Do not wait for CT.** |
| **Severe proptosis** | Proptosis >5mm (using Hertel) or inability to close eyelids | Urgent ophthalmology review – may need canthotomy. |
| **No improvement after 48h IV antibiotics** | Persistent fever, no decrease in CRP, worsening proptosis | Repeat CT; consider surgical drainage (ophthalmology + ENT). |
| **Suspected cavernous sinus thrombosis** | New cranial nerve palsy (any of III, IV, VI), bilateral signs, seizures, GCS <15 | **Call ICU.** Start IV heparin (if no contraindication), broad-spectrum antibiotics, neuroimaging (MRI/MRV). |
| **Meningism / altered consciousness** | Neck stiffness, photophobia, GCS drop | **Call ICU and neurology.** Lumbar puncture after CT. Consider starting ceftriaxone + vancomycin. |
| **NEWS2 score** | ≥5 (e.g., RR>24, SpO2<92%, HR>110, SBP<90) | Escalate to medical/surgical registrar. |
| **Lactate** | >2.0 mmol/L with signs of sepsis | Sepsis 6 bundle; inform ICU if >4. |
| **GCS** | ≤13 or drop by 2 points | **Crash call** – possible raised ICP or intracranial complication. |

**Bottom line for the FY1:** If you see a child with periorbital swelling and any hint of restricted eye movement or proptosis, you do not send them home with oral antibiotics. You start IV antibiotics, get a CT, and speak to ophthalmology. That single decision changes outcome.
